text: The surgeon infiltrates the operative area with a local anesthetic and vasoconstrictor, aiming to achieve a bloodless field for the anal surgery.
The surgeon places a Lone Star retractor after making a skin incision, effectively opening the wound for better visibility.
The surgeon performs further dissection using scissors or a cold scalpel, carefully separating tissue to minimize additional damage.
The surgeon identifies the edges of the torn sphincter and gently mobilizes them, ensuring the scar tissue remains intact.
Great attention should be given not to damage anoderm or vaginal mucosa.
The surgeon mobilizes and blocks each edge of the sphincter without separately identifying the internal and external sphincters, ensuring the edges are prepared for further surgical intervention.
Fibrotic tissue should not be excised.
The surgeon performs full-length mobilization of the sphincter's fibrotic ends, extending the dissection up to the levator muscle as necessary.
The surgeon carefully performs lateral dissection of the sphincter, taking precautions to avoid damaging the surrounding anal nerves.
The surgeon focuses on the anatomical structures located at the three and nine o'clock positions, likely referring to specific areas around the anal sphincter.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the sphincter, ensuring that the muscle edges overlap by at least two centimeters.
The surgeon begins muscle suturing by placing overlapping U-stitches using absorbable monofilament sutures on the mobilized sphincter muscle.
The surgeon carefully ties knots in the sutures to prevent ischemic damage to the muscle, ensuring secure closure without compromising blood flow.
Levatorplasty might be added if needed.
The surgeon closes the skin wound vertically, enhancing the newly created perineal body.
Usually there is no need for drainage.
